elements.sent-2411-001: Quantum computing solutions are increasingly deployed in commercial environments through delegated computing, especially one of the most critical issues is to guarantee the confidentiality and proprietary of quantum implementations.
elements.sent-2411-002: Since the proposal of general-purpose indistinguishability obfuscation (iO) and functional encryption schemes, iO has emerged as a seemingly versatile cryptography primitive.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing research on quantum indistinguishable obfuscation (QiO) primarily focuses on task-oriented, lacking solutions to general quantum computing.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose a scheme for constructing QiO via the equivalence of quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-005: It introduces the concept of quantum subpath sum equivalence, demonstrating that indistinguishability between two quantum circuits can be achieved by incremental changes in quantum subpaths.
elements.sent-2411-006: The restriction of security loss is solved by reducing the distinguisher to polynomial probability test.
elements.sent-2411-007: The scheme obfuscates the quantum implementation of classical functions in a path-sum specification, ensuring the indistinguishability between different quantum implementations.
elements.sent-2411-008: The results demonstrate the feasibility of indistinguishability obfuscation for general circuits and provide novel insights on intellectual property protection and secure delegated quantum computing.
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, quantum computing has emerged as a transformative technology with the potential to revolutionize various fields, including scientific research, optimization, and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-010: As quantum computing solutions are increasingly deployed in commercial settings, one of the foremost concerns is to guarantee the confidentiality and proprietary of quantum circuits, particularly in the context of delegated quantum computing.
elements.sent-2411-011: The circuits encapsulate complex algorithms, sensitive data, and proprietary functionalities essential for executing various computational tasks.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is crucial to ensure the integrity and confidentiality of quantum circuits for safeguarding intellectual property, trade secrets, and sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-013: The concept of obfuscation originated from program obfuscation: software companies aim to prevent users from obtaining the implementation logic of the program through decompilation and other methods after releasing their software, thereby safeguarding their trade secrets.
elements.sent-2411-014: The theoretical foundation of program obfuscation was proposed in 2001 \cite{2001Barak , and it was not until 2013 that researchers put forward schemes for obfuscating general computing \cite{2013Garg .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A parallel line of research over the last decade has demonstrated that most cryptographic primitives, including several powerful ones such as multiparty non-interactive key exchange \cite{BZ14 , succinct non-interactive arguments \cite{SW21 , software watermarking \cite{2016stoc , and deniable encryption \cite{SW21 can be built from indistinguishability obfuscation (iO).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, iO has also found appeal outside cryptography, e.g. for establishing the hardness of certain tasks in differential privacy \cite{BZ14 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: These results have established iO as a `central hub' of theoretical cryptography.

elements.sent-2411-018: Similar to classical obfuscation, quantum obfuscation provides a viable technological solution to guaranteeing secure delegated quantum computing while protecting the execution logic of commercially sensitive quantum programs from potential adversary.
elements.sent-2411-019: The characteristics of quantum entanglement and superposition hinder direct application of classical obfuscation in quantum obfuscation.
elements.sent-2411-020: Research on quantum obfuscation has predominantly focused on definitional work \cite{AF16 , impossibility results \cite{AF16,ABDS21 , and limited classes of quantum computing \cite{Shang19,Zhang22,BK21,BM22 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: One prominent framework for quantum obfuscation is Virtual Black Box (VBB) obfuscation.
elements.sent-2411-022: The idea behind VBB is that an obfuscated program should behave like a black box when observed by any adversarial party, ensuring that regardless of the adversary’s observations, analyses, or queries, they cannot extract more information than what a direct query to the original program’s oracle would reveal.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, achieving VBB obfuscation for general quantum circuits has proven challenging.
elements.sent-2411-024: Alagic et al. \cite{ABDS21 demonstrated the provable existence of circuits that cannot be VBB obfuscation, even when utilizing quantum information.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, subsequent research on quantum obfuscation has primarily adopted two approaches, one involves constructing VBB obfuscation for limited classes of quantum computing, and the other focuses on relaxation of VBB obfuscation towards general-purpose.
elements.sent-2411-026: Recent research has explored VBB obfuscation under certain restricted scenarios, such as quantum point function obfuscation \cite{Shang19,Zhang21 and its instantiation schemes \cite{Zhang22 , black-box constructions for unlearnable programs \cite{ALL+21 , polynomial-size pseudo-deterministic quantum circuit \cite{2023Pseudo , quantum non-linear functions \cite{Pan2023 , have demonstrated its viability under specific conditions.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Unlike the limited classes of quantum computing for VBB obfuscation research above, Barak et al. \cite{BGI+12 introduced a relaxation concept of obfuscation known as indistinguishability obfuscation, which only requires that the obfuscation of two functionally equivalent programs is indistinguishable in computing.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Despite this relaxation, subsequent works such as \cite{GGH+16,SW21 demonstrated its considerable strength.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Quantum indistinguishability obfuscation (QiO) possesses the potential to construct various other quantum cryptographic primitives and can be applied to a wider range of quantum computing tasks and application scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, as shown in Table ~\ref{tab:qio_comparison , designing QiO schemes for general-purpose quantum computing remains challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-031: As far as we know about the existing constructions from the literature, such as those by Broadbent et al. \cite{BK21 , Bartusek et al. \cite{BM22 , and Coladangelo et al. \cite{2023How , constraint QiO to restricted classes of quantum circuits, such as those using limited non-Clifford gates, trivial output functions, or a fixed universal quantum circuit for best-possible copy protection.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Instead of considering implementations as circuit-state pairs, the description of circuit is assumed to be included in state.
elements.sent-2411-033: Despite their theoretical importance, these constructions fall short of addressing the complexities inherent in general quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-034: Additionally, proving functional equivalence for general quantum circuits often requires exhaustive enumeration of all possible inputs.
elements.sent-2411-035: This leads to exponential security loss, making such schemes impractical for large-scale or complex circuits.
elements.sent-2411-036: Even the best current constructions rely on inefficient exhaustive verification, which scales poorly and restricts their applicability to limited scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this paper, we propose a scheme aimed at constructing QiO by leveraging the equivalence proof of quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-038: The starting point is a simple observation: assuming the existence of a secure quantum indistinguishability obfuscator, a mathematical proof is developed to convince oneself and others that two programs are functionally equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-039: We introduce a polynomial-time probabilistic test for functional equivalence, significantly mitigating the exponential security loss inherent in previous approaches.
elements.sent-2411-040: By hybrid argument, we demonstrate that two quantum implementations of classical functions are quantum indistinguishable.
elements.sent-2411-041: The main contributions of this paper is described as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-042: We overcome the constraints of existing QiO schemes that apply only to certain classes of quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-043: Through semantic transformations of quantum circuits, we construct a general-purpose QiO scheme that works for arbitrary quantum circuits, bypassing the need for restrictive gate classes and trivial output functions.
elements.sent-2411-044: It also eliminates the restrictive assumptions of previous QiO constructions and solves the problem of exponential security loss.
elements.sent-2411-045: We propose QiO scheme via quantum circuit equivalence (QceQiO).
elements.sent-2411-046: It is the candidate construction of indistinguishable obfuscation for general quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-047: It relies on the reverse engineering of circuit equivalence through succinct mathematical proofs, enabling efficient and scalable obfuscation for arbitrary quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-048: We introduce the concept of incremental subpath equivalence, which involves transforming quantum circuits through incremental changes in subpaths.
elements.sent-2411-049: Two circuits only differ in two polynomial-sized functionally equivalent subpaths at the same position, and each incremental change is of small scale.
elements.sent-2411-050: In addition, the problem of distinguishing quantum circuits is translated into a polynomial identity testing problem.

elements.section-2411-003: Quantum Indistinguishable Obfuscation Scheme via Quantum Circuit Equivalence
elements.sent-2411-097: In this section, we provide the definition and construction of quantum indistinguishability obfuscation scheme via quantum circuit equivalence (QceQiO).
elements.sent-2411-098: A framework of quantum indistinguishable obfuscation scheme is shown in Fig. \ref{fig13 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: A function $f$ take semantic transformation to generate a quantum specification comprising the quantum implementation.
elements.sent-2411-100: This specification is then represented as subpath sums, forming the basis for subsequent transformations.
elements.sent-2411-101: By introducing incremental changes in subpath equivalence, QceQiO progressively constructs obfuscated circuits, preserving functional equivalence while ensuring indistinguishability.
elements.sent-2411-102: Polynomial identity testing is applied to the phase polynomials of quantum subpaths, reducing the distinguisher to a polynomial probability test.
elements.sent-2411-103: Before presenting quantum indistinguishability obfuscation for equivalent quantum circuits, we provide the definition of an equivalent quantum implementation of a function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The quantum implementation of a classical function is defined as follows \cite{2023How :.}
elements.sent-2411-105: A $(1-\epsilon)$-quantum implementation of $f$ is a pair $(\rho,C)$, where $\rho$ is an initial quantum state, $C$ is a quantum circuit acting on $\rho$ and an additional $n$-qubit register initialized with the classical input $x$.
elements.sent-2411-106: The implementation satisfies: \begin{equation}\forall x \in \left\{ {0,~1} \right\}^{n},\Pr \left\lbrack C(\rho,~x) = f(x) \right\rbrack \geq 1 - \epsilon.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-107: The size of the quantum implementation is defined as the maximum of the number of qubits in $\rho$ and the number of gates in $C$.
elements.sent-2411-108: In contrast to the classical circuit model, quantum gates in a quantum circuit are restricted to a subset of all operators on $\mathcal{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-109: Specifically, quantum gates are linear operators $\left.
elements.sent-2411-111: Such operators $U$ satisfy $U^{\dagger U = UU^{\dagger = ~I$, where $U^{\dagger}$ denotes the adjoint of $U$, and they are referred to as unitary operators.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Quantum circuits essentially describe the action of unitary operators on a Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-113: In quantum computing, equivalent quantum implementations can perform the same quantum computing functionality, which means that copy protection generates outputs will be indistinguishable when equivalent quantum implementations are input.
elements.sent-2411-114: This is a fundamental approach for copy protection of quantum implementations.
elements.sent-2411-115: In recent work of Andrea et al. \cite{2023How , the approach is also referred to as the best possible copy protection.}
elements.sent-2411-116: We present the definition of QceQiO.
elements.sent-2411-117: A quantum indistinguishability obfuscator for equivalent quantum circuits is a quantum polynomial-time (QPT) algorithm QceQiO that takes as input a security parameter $1^{\lambda}$ and a pair of quantum implementations $(\rho,C) \in Q_{\lambda}$, and outputs a pair of $\left( \rho^{\prime ,C^{\prime \right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Additionally, QceQiO should satisfy the following conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-119: Polynomial Expansion: There exists a polynomial function $\text{poly (n)$ such that for all $C \in \mathcal{C}$, $\mathcal{C}$ is quantum circuit family ,the size of the obfuscated circuit $C^{\prime}$ satisfies: \begin{equation}|C'| = \text{poly}(|C|).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-120: This means that the size of the obfuscated circuit $C^{\prime}$ is polynomially bounded in terms of the size of $C$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Functional equivalence: For any $C \in \mathcal{C}$, \begin{equation}(\rho', C') \leftarrow QceQiO(\rho, C) , \nonumber C  and  C'  are under  \Delta  subpath equivalence.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}(\rho', C') \leftarrow QceQiO(\rho, C) , \nonumber C  and  C'  are under  \Delta  subpath equivalence.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-122: Computational indistinguishability: For any QPT distinguisher $D$, there exists a negligible function $negl$ such that for all $\lambda$ and two pairs of quantum implementations $(\rho_{1 , C_{1 ), (\rho_{2 , C_{2 )$ of the same function $f$, the distributions of the obfuscated outputs are computationally indistinguishable: \begin{equation}0.47\textwidth{!}{ $\left| \begin{matrix} {{\Pr\left\lbrack {D\left( ~QceQiO\left( {1^{\lambda},~\left( {\rho_1,C_1} \right)} \right)\rightarrow\left( {\rho_{1}^{\prime},C_{1}^{\prime}} \right) \right) = 1} \right\rbrack} -} {\Pr\left\lbrack {D\left( QceQiO\left( {1^{\lambda},~\left( {\rho_2,C_2} \right)} \right)\rightarrow\left( {\rho_{2}^{\prime},C_{2}^{\prime}} \right) \right) = 1} \right\rbrack} \end{matrix} \right| \leq ~negl(\lambda).$}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Similar to the indistinguishability of quantum states represented by density operators, distinguishing the equivalence of two quantum implementations of classical function $f$ can be simplified.
elements.sent-2411-124: Simplification involves sequentially evolving all inputs using the constructed unitary matrices.
elements.sent-2411-125: This is also an implicit strategy in the security proofs of all universal iO structures.
elements.sent-2411-126: Although conceptually straightforward, it quickly becomes a challenging task due to the exponential scaling of matrices with the number of qubits \cite{1993bern .}
elements.sent-2411-127: It is also an implicit security loss in all indistinguishability obfuscation.
elements.sent-2411-128: As seen from the definition of QiO, if two quantum circuits $C_1$ and $C_2$ exhibit functional equivalence, i.e., for all inputs $x$, $C_{1 (x) = C_{2 (x)$, then their obfuscations must be indistinguishable for any polynomial-time algorithm.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Assuming there exists a polynomial-time reduction that, given access to the oracle of a QiO scheme, breaks the assumed security based on some polynomial-time hardness assumption.
elements.sent-2411-130: Consider a `trivial' polynomial-time adversary $A$ that selects two quantum circuits $C_{1 (x) = C_{2 (x)$, which are functionally equivalent except for differing outputs at some input $x^{*}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: This adversary can distinguish the obfuscations of $C_1$ and $C_2$ by evaluating them at $x^{*}$.
elements.sent-2411-132: To prevent from being fooled by adversary $A$, it is necessary to check whether the two quantum circuits $C_1$ and $C_2$ are functionally equivalent for all inputs.
elements.sent-2411-133: A natural approach is to traverse all inputs one by one, which is also the implicit strategy in the security proofs of all existing generic QiO constructions.
elements.sent-2411-134: Consider a simple scenario where the outputs of two quantum circuits $C_1$ and $C_2$ differ only at a specific $x^{*}$ for all inputs.
elements.sent-2411-135: If the input space is two-dimensional, traversing all possible inputs one by one requires checking $2^n$ different inputs, where $n$ is the input length, leading to exponential computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-136: This strategy has also been named by Jain et al. \cite{2022jain as suffering from the security loss with input length increases exponentially.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Therefore, in the design of QiO, checking functional equivalence is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-138: Without such verification, it is impossible to ensure consistent obfuscation in terms of functionality.

elements.sent-2411-283: In this section, we present the security analysis of QceQiO.
elements.sent-2411-284: The primary tools for the proof include the `Clifford twirl' \cite{ABOEM17 and the `admissible oracle lemma' \cite{GJMZ23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-285: The `Clifford twirl' is sufficient to establish security against adversaries performing a single query, but a more intricate argument is required to handle general adversaries.
elements.sent-2411-286: This argument leverages the `admissible oracle lemma'.
elements.sent-2411-287: We prove that the two quantum implementations of the classical function $f$ using QceQiO are indistinguishable in the quantum random oracle model.
elements.sent-2411-288: This result aligns with previous research in the field, which has extensively examined the security of quantum-cryptographic constructions in random oracle model \cite{Zhang22,AC12,BM22 .}
elements.sent-2411-289: In this section, we consider a classical scenario in quantum cloud delegation computation \cite{Verification19 to illustrate the practical setting for QceQiO’s obfuscation.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Quantum delegation protocols typically involve two parties: a verifier and a prover.
elements.sent-2411-291: The verifier’s goal is to delegate the execution of a complex quantum circuit $C$ to a prover while ensuring that the computation is performed correctly and securely.
